findings.title: Singapore FATCA/CRS Reporting Deadline: June 1, 2026 — Today
findings.summary: Singapore Reporting Financial Institutions must submit FATCA and CRS returns covering 2025 data to IRAS by May 31, 2026; as May 31 falls on a Sunday, the effective deadline is June 1, 2026. Failure to comply risks monetary fines and, under FATCA, a 30% withholding tax on US-sourced payments.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding accounts at Singapore financial institutions will have their 2025 account data reported to IRAS and exchanged with their home-country tax authorities today; clients with undisclosed offshore income or accounts should be aware that this information is now entering the exchange pipeline.
findings.urgency_reason: Filing deadline is today, June 1, 2026. Reporting institutions must have filed; any that have not are immediately non-compliant.

findings.title: CRS 2.0 / DAC8: FATCA/CRS Filing Deadline June 1, 2026 Across Nine Jurisdictions — Today
findings.summary: CRS 2.0 (aligned with EU DAC8) is effective January 1, 2026, expanding the Common Reporting Standard to include digital assets. The May 31, 2026 FATCA/CRS filing deadline — effective June 1 where May 31 is a Sunday — applies to BVI, Anguilla, Bahrain, India, Jamaica, Singapore, Spain, UK, and South Africa for 2025-year reports.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding accounts in any of these nine jurisdictions will have their 2025 financial data — now expanded under CRS 2.0 to include digital asset holdings — reported and exchanged with their home-country tax authorities today; clients with crypto or digital asset positions at reporting financial institutions should expect those holdings to appear in CRS reports for the first time this cycle.
findings.urgency_reason: Multi-jurisdiction FATCA/CRS filing deadline is today, June 1, 2026; information is entering the automatic exchange pipeline now and clients' home-country tax authorities will receive it.

findings.title: BVI CRS 2.0: May 31, 2026 FATCA/CRS Filing Deadline Passed Yesterday
findings.summary: BVI implemented CRS 2.0 effective January 1, 2026, expanding CRS scope to include digital assets. The May 31, 2026 deadline for submission of 2025-year CRS/FATCA reports to the BVI ITA passed yesterday. For 2026-year data under CRS 2.0, submission to BVI ITA is due May 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with BVI financial accounts had their 2025 data — including digital asset holdings under CRS 2.0 — due for submission yesterday; clients whose BVI trustees or administrators may have missed the deadline face regulatory exposure requiring immediate remediation before penalties are assessed.
findings.urgency_reason: The May 31, 2026 filing deadline passed yesterday; any BVI reporting financial institution that did not file is now in breach and requires immediate remediation action.

findings.title: Luxembourg DAC9 Law: Pillar Two GIR Filing and Registration Due June 30, 2026 — 29 Days
findings.summary: Luxembourg enacted its DAC9 Law on December 17, 2025, transposing EU Directive 2025/872 on exchange of GloBE Information Returns under Pillar Two. The first GIR and Pillar Two registration for Luxembourg entities in MNE groups in scope from January 1, 2024 is due June 30, 2026. Luxembourg consolidated QDMTT data into the GIR, eliminating a separate QDMTT filing.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Luxembourg entities in MNE groups with consolidated revenues above €750M must complete Pillar Two registration and file the GIR by June 30, 2026 — just 29 days away — or face penalties; FGC should verify with all affected clients that filing preparations are complete and advisors engaged.
findings.urgency_reason: Hard filing deadline of June 30, 2026 is 29 days from today; calendar-year MNE groups with Luxembourg entities in scope of Pillar Two must act immediately.

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two: First GIR Filings Due June 30, 2026 — 29 Days Remaining
findings.summary: On May 18, 2026, the OECD published Administrative Guidance allowing late-filing penalty waivers where an MNE centrally files the GIR in an operationally ready jurisdiction even if exchange relationships are not yet activated (excluding Bahamas, North Macedonia, Slovakia, and Vietnam). First GIR filings for calendar-year taxpayers are due June 30, 2026 across all implementing jurisdictions.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are owners or beneficiaries of MNE groups with €750M+ consolidated revenues face a firm June 30, 2026 GIR filing deadline across all Pillar Two jurisdictions where group entities are located; the new OECD penalty-waiver guidance provides potential relief if the group files centrally in a ready jurisdiction, but this must be arranged immediately.
findings.urgency_reason: Global Pillar Two GIR deadline is June 30, 2026, exactly 29 days away; multi-jurisdictional coordination is required and time is critically short.

findings.title: BCB Resolution 561: Stablecoin and Crypto Banned from eFX Cross-Border Settlements — Effective October 1, 2026
findings.summary: Banco Central do Brasil Resolution BCB No. 561 (April 30, 2026) prohibits eFX providers from using stablecoins (USDT, USDC) or other virtual assets to settle cross-border payments. Effective October 1, 2026; authorized institutions must update Unicad registration by October 30, 2026; unauthorized firms have until May 31, 2027 to obtain BCB approval.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian HNW clients or their family businesses using crypto or stablecoin rails for cross-border remittances must transition to traditional FX channels or non-resident BRL accounts by October 1, 2026 — four months away — or face regulatory sanctions under the BCB framework.
findings.urgency_reason: Effective October 1, 2026 gives clients approximately four months to convert payment infrastructure; the 30-day URGENT threshold is not yet met but the deadline is near enough to require active planning now.

findings.title: BCB Travel Rule Updated for VASPs in Cross-Border Flows — Effective May 2026
findings.summary: Alongside Resolution 561, Brazil tightened Travel Rule obligations for VASPs in May 2026, requiring originator/beneficiary information transmittal for qualifying virtual asset cross-border transfers. Individual investors may still hold crypto, but the eFX remittance channel is now closed to non-BCB-authorized VASPs.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident HNW clients conducting cross-border value transfers through virtual asset service providers must ensure those providers hold BCB authorization and comply with Travel Rule data-sharing requirements, or restructure remittance arrangements before the October 2026 cut-off.
findings.urgency_reason: Rule is already in effect as of May 2026; no single 30-day deadline, but any current arrangement using a non-authorized VASP for cross-border flows is non-compliant and requires prompt review.

findings.title: CVM 2026 Regulatory Agenda: Resolution 175 Amendments and Crowdfunding Overhaul Underway
findings.summary: CVM's 2026 agenda prioritizes amendments to Resolution 175 covering FIPs, FIIs, FIFs, actively managed ETFs, and FIDCs exposed to distressed credit, plus a redesigned crowdfunding framework to replace Resolution 88. No enacted dates confirmed; consultations are ongoing.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Brazilian investment fund structures — particularly FIPs and FIIs — should anticipate regulatory changes to operational and disclosure requirements under Resolution 175 later in 2026; revised crowdfunding rules may also open new alternative investment access channels for eligible investors.
findings.urgency_reason: No hard deadline; ongoing consultations mean rule changes could be enacted at any point in 2026 and affected fund structures require continuous monitoring.

findings.title: LC 227/2026: Progressive ITCMD Mandatory Nationwide — Offshore Trust Exposure Heightened
findings.summary: Complementary Law 227/2026 mandates progressive ITCMD inheritance and gift tax rates (2%–8%) across all Brazilian states, eliminating flat-rate structures. Combined with Law 14,754/2023 treatment of offshore trust assets, Brazilian-resident settlors of offshore trusts now face layered ITCMD and income-tax exposure.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident HNW clients with offshore trusts or estate plans relying on flat-rate state ITCMD must review their structures promptly, as LC 227/2026 combined with Law 14,754/2023 materially increases the tax cost of holding and transferring wealth through offshore arrangements and may make domestic holding structures more attractive.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is enacted and applies in 2026 with no single action deadline, but the structural change in ITCMD rates means clients with existing offshore trust or estate structures need a tax review soon to assess and potentially restructure.

findings.title: FinCEN BOI Interim Final Rule: Domestic Entities Exempt; Foreign-Registered Entities Must Report Within 30 Days of Registration
findings.summary: FinCEN's Interim Final Rule (March 26, 2025) exempts all US-formed entities from BOI reporting under the CTA. Only foreign-law entities registering to do business in a US State must report; those registering on or after March 26, 2025 must file within 30 days of effective registration. A final rule is expected in 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using foreign entities (Cayman, BVI, Luxembourg, etc.) that register to conduct US business face a rolling 30-day BOI filing obligation from each registration event; missing this window carries civil and criminal penalties under the Corporate Transparency Act.
findings.urgency_reason: No single upcoming deadline, but the 30-day clock starts on registration and applies on a continuous basis; FGC must track all new US State registrations of foreign client entities to ensure timely filing.

findings.title: Digital Asset Market Clarity Act Advances in Senate; White House Targets July 4, 2026 Passage
findings.summary: The US Senate Banking Committee voted 15-9 on May 14, 2026 to advance the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, described as the most significant financial regulatory restructuring since Dodd-Frank. The bill clarifies SEC/CFTC jurisdiction over digital assets, and the White House has set a July 4, 2026 passage target.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with digital asset portfolios or investments in crypto funds should track this legislation closely; if enacted, it will resolve longstanding regulatory uncertainty over the securities vs. commodities classification of digital assets, potentially triggering fund registration, compliance, or portfolio restructuring requirements.
findings.urgency_reason: Bill not yet law but White House July 4, 2026 target means regulatory clarity could arrive within 33 days, potentially requiring rapid action on fund structure and digital asset custody arrangements.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 Foreign Buyer Restrictions Upheld: Affidavit Mandatory; Criminal Penalties for Non-Compliance
findings.summary: Florida SB 264 (effective July 1, 2023) was upheld by courts in November 2025. Chinese nationals face a near-total ban on purchasing any Florida real estate; nationals of Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, and Syria face restrictions near military or critical infrastructure. All buyers must sign a compliance affidavit; violations risk property forfeiture and criminal charges.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients from China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, or Syria seeking to acquire Florida real estate face categorical legal barriers or geographic restrictions; FGC must conduct nationality screening on all Florida property transactions and ensure affidavit compliance to protect clients from forfeiture and criminal liability.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is upheld and fully in force; the forfeiture and criminal consequences justify active monitoring for any affected client pursuing Florida real estate even though there is no new legislative deadline.

findings.title: Cayman Tokenised Funds Framework in Force Since March 24, 2026: Mutual Funds and Private Funds Acts Amended
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 and Private Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 came into force March 24, 2026, creating a comprehensive framework for tokenised funds regulated identically to traditional funds. Issuance of digital equity/investment tokens by tokenised funds is excluded from the VASP regime.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients and family offices investing in or establishing Cayman-domiciled tokenised funds now have a clear regulatory framework; existing Cayman funds that have issued or are considering digital tokens should confirm their structure falls within the new Acts and not the VASP regime to avoid dual regulatory obligations.
findings.urgency_reason: Framework has been in force since March 24, 2026; no immediate deadline, but structures using digital tokens should be reviewed for compliance with the new Acts as a matter of priority.

findings.title: Cayman BO Discrepancy Notification Shortened to 5 Days; Voluntary-Cure Penalty Reduction Abolished
findings.summary: The Cayman Beneficial Ownership Transparency (Amendment) Regulations, 2026 (January 23, 2026) shortened the discrepancy notification deadline from 30 days to 5 days and abolished the 25% administrative penalty reduction for voluntary cure of filing breaches. New fees for legitimate-interest access were also introduced.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Cayman entities must ensure their registered agents and directors can identify and report beneficial ownership discrepancies within 5 business days of discovery — a dramatically tighter window that significantly increases operational compliance risk and removes the prior incentive for self-correction.
findings.urgency_reason: Regulations are in force since January 2026; the 5-day window requires immediate review of BO record-keeping and monitoring processes for all Cayman structures to avoid penalties that can no longer be reduced by voluntary cure.

findings.title: BVI Remains on FATF Grey List and EU AML High-Risk List; Enhanced CDD Required for EU Transactions
findings.summary: BVI was added to the FATF list of Jurisdictions Under Increased Monitoring in June 2025 and remains listed as of the February 2026 Plenary. The EU Commission added BVI to its AML high-risk list in December 2025, requiring enhanced CDD for EU-counterparty transactions involving BVI entities. BVI projects completing FATF requirements by mid-2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using BVI entities in transactions with European counterparties, banks, or investors face enhanced due diligence requirements and potential banking friction; FGC should advise clients on the heightened compliance burden and assess whether alternative jurisdictions offer equivalent functionality with lower AML risk.
findings.urgency_reason: BVI grey-list and EU high-risk status are ongoing with no removal expected before mid-2027; clients should be briefed and all BVI structures reviewed for the practical impact of the enhanced CDD obligation on banking and investment relationships.

findings.title: BVI Beneficial Ownership 'Legitimate Interest' Access Portal Open from April 1, 2026
findings.summary: From April 1, 2026, the BVI Registry accepts applications from journalists, civil society organisations, financial crime investigators, and potential business counterparties to access beneficial ownership information. Access is limited to UBOs holding 25% or more ownership or equivalent control. The register is not publicly accessible.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are UBOs of BVI entities should be aware that their identity is now accessible — via a formal application process — to journalists, NGOs, and counterparties with demonstrated legitimate interest; this represents a material reduction in BVI confidentiality for qualifying ownership stakes and requires that filed BO information is accurate.
findings.urgency_reason: Portal opened April 1, 2026; no immediate deadline, but clients should review their BVI ownership disclosures for accuracy since erroneous information is now more likely to be identified and challenged by third-party access.

findings.title: Luxembourg Carried Interest Tax Reform Effective FY2026: Full Exemption for Qualifying AIF Participations
findings.summary: Luxembourg's Parliament adopted Bill 8590 on January 22, 2026, exempting from tax carried interest linked to AIF participations held over six months where the participation does not exceed 10% of fund share capital. The prior requirement that carried interest can only be paid after full investor capital recovery is abolished.
findings.client_impact: Fund managers and HNW clients receiving carried interest from Luxembourg-based AIFs may now structure their arrangements to qualify for a full tax exemption, removing a significant tax cost, provided the 6-month hold and 10% cap conditions are satisfied; existing arrangements should be reviewed for restructuring opportunities.
findings.urgency_reason: Effective FY2026; no single action deadline, but existing carried interest arrangements should be reviewed against the new conditions to determine whether they qualify for the exemption or can be restructured to do so.

findings.title: Luxembourg SPF Can Now Be Formed as Simplified Joint-Stock Company (SAS) — Effective Early 2026
findings.summary: The Luxembourg law of December 19, 2025 amended the SPF law (May 11, 2007) to permit private wealth management companies to be established as sociétés par actions simplifiées (SAS), enhancing governance and capital flexibility for HNW family wealth structures in Luxembourg.
findings.client_impact: HNW families using or planning Luxembourg SPFs now have the option of an SAS structure with greater flexibility in governance arrangements, potentially making Luxembourg a more attractive jurisdiction for dynastic wealth holding relative to alternatives such as Jersey or Liechtenstein.
findings.urgency_reason: New structuring option available from early 2026; no compliance deadline, but clients planning new Luxembourg wealth vehicles should evaluate the SAS form as part of the initial structuring analysis.

findings.title: Swiss LETA + Revised AMLA to Enter Force H2 2026: AML Obligations Extend to Lawyers, Trustees, and Advisors
findings.summary: Switzerland's Parliament passed the Federal Act on the Transparency of Legal Entities (LETA) and a revised Anti-Money Laundering Act on September 26, 2025, both expected to enter into force in H2 2026. LETA creates a centralized federal beneficial ownership register accessible to competent authorities. The revised AMLA extends AML/CDD obligations to lawyers, notaries, trustees, and corporate service providers for advisory, structuring, real estate, and entity formation work.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients relying on Swiss lawyers, fiduciaries, or trustees for wealth structuring and entity formation will face enhanced due diligence and disclosure obligations once LETA and the revised AMLA enter force — expected within the next 1–6 months; existing Swiss structures may require review to satisfy the new beneficial ownership standards before the effective date.
findings.urgency_reason: Entry into force is H2 2026, meaning changes could arrive within approximately 1–6 months; FGC and clients with Swiss advisory relationships should begin compliance preparation now.

findings.title: Switzerland Implementing CARF: Crypto-Asset Reporting Begins for Calendar Year 2026; First Exchange in 2027
findings.summary: Switzerland is implementing the OECD Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework under its AEOI legislation. The first reportable period is calendar year 2026, with the first exchange of information with foreign competent authorities in 2027. This aligns with a concurrent FATCA Schema v2.0.1 update.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding crypto assets at Swiss financial institutions — exchanges, brokers, custody providers — should expect those holdings to be reported to Swiss authorities and automatically exchanged with their home-country tax administrations in 2027, making Swiss crypto accounts fully transparent from the 2026 tax year; this requires clients to ensure their tax positions are consistent with forthcoming disclosure.
findings.urgency_reason: The 2026 calendar year is the first CARF reportable period; while exchange occurs in 2027, reporting institutions are collecting data now and clients must act on any tax position inconsistencies before year-end.

findings.title: UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 10/2025: New AML Law in Force Since October 2025 — Tax Evasion Now a Predicate Offence
findings.summary: UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 10/2025 (in force October 14, 2025) introduces a standalone proliferation financing offence, expands AML predicate offences to include tax evasion, adopts an objective knowledge standard removing the need to prove knowledge of the specific predicate, expressly covers digital assets, and empowers the FIU to suspend transactions for up to 10 days and freeze assets for up to 30 days without a prior conviction.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with UAE entities or banking relationships must be aware that tax evasion is now an AML predicate offence — a significant escalation — and that the FIU can freeze assets pre-conviction; undisclosed or aggressively structured tax positions connected to UAE structures now carry a materially elevated legal risk.
findings.urgency_reason: Law has been in force since October 2025; while there is no immediate compliance deadline, the expanded predicate offence scope and pre-emptive asset-freezing powers represent a permanently elevated enforcement environment that must be factored into all UAE-connected structuring and tax advice.

findings.title: UAE UBO Regime: Nominee Structures Formally Defined; 15-Day Update Obligation Operative 2026
findings.summary: UAE Cabinet Decision No. 109/2023 (operative 2026) formally defines Nominee Shareholders and Directors; nominees must disclose their status and nominator identity to the company within 15 working days of any change. Companies must update UBO records within 15 working days of any change under an 'Adequate, Accurate, Up-to-Date' information standard.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using UAE entities with nominee arrangements must ensure nominees have formally disclosed their status and nominator identity on record, and that UBO registers are updated within 15 working days of any ownership change — failure to comply under the new AML law can attract fines up to AED 5 million.
findings.urgency_reason: Regime is operative in 2026; the 15-working-day update clock is self-executing and rolling, meaning any recent change in UAE entity ownership or nominee arrangements must be immediately verified for compliance.

findings.title: Panama MLI Synthesized Texts Published: Principal Purpose Test Now In Force Across 16 Treaties
findings.summary: Panama published synthesized texts for 16 of its 17 MLI-modified tax treaties on March 30, 2026. All 16 now include the BEPS Principal Purpose Test (PPT), enabling competent authorities to deny treaty benefits where obtaining those benefits was a principal purpose of an arrangement. Treaty partners include Luxembourg, Netherlands, Spain, UK, UAE, and Singapore.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using Panama holding structures to route income through treaty partners face material treaty-access risk under the PPT; arrangements designed primarily to capture treaty benefits — rather than driven by genuine commercial substance in Panama — are now legally challengeable by counterparty tax authorities.
findings.urgency_reason: PPT is now in force across Panama's 16 MLI-modified treaties; structures relying on treaty benefits should be reviewed for substance and business purpose before the next income-payment or asset disposition event.

findings.title: Cyprus Tax Reform Effective 2026: Corporate Rate Rises to 15%; SDC on Dividends Reduced to 5%
findings.summary: Cyprus increased its corporate income tax rate from 12.5% to 15% (aligning with Pillar Two DMTT, also implemented from 2025) and reduced the Special Defence Contribution on dividends paid to Cyprus tax-resident and domiciled individuals from 17% to 5%; the SDC rate for all other cases remains at 0%.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Cyprus holding companies or SPVs will pay a higher corporate rate on retained profits, but Cyprus-resident beneficiaries receiving dividends face substantially reduced SDC — a significant incentive to book dividends in 2026; overall, Cyprus remains competitive among EU holding jurisdictions, though the rate increase warrants a review of holding structure economics.
findings.urgency_reason: Effective for fiscal year 2026; no single action deadline, but clients with Cyprus structures should review dividend policy and holding company models against the changed rate combination before year-end.

findings.title: Bahamas CFATF 5th Round Mutual Evaluation On-Site Visit: October 19-30, 2026; Six Laws Tightened
findings.summary: The Bahamas faces a CFATF 5th Round Mutual Evaluation on-site visit on October 19-30, 2026. Six laws — including the Proceeds of Crime Act and Register of Beneficial Ownership Act — have been amended. New Central Bank guidelines for Private Trust Companies, Qualified Executive Entities, and Registered Representatives were published following a December 2025 public consultation.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Bahamas-based trusts, PTCs, or fund structures should expect heightened compliance scrutiny and potentially increased administrative requirements as Bahamas service providers tighten standards before the October evaluation; structures should be reviewed to ensure they meet the newly updated beneficial ownership and AML requirements.
findings.urgency_reason: On-site evaluation is October 2026; service providers are actively increasing compliance standards now, which may affect onboarding timelines, CDD depth, and administrative costs for clients' Bahamas structures.

findings.title: Trusts (Jersey) Eighth Amendment in Force March 20, 2026: Beneficiary Termination Rights Restricted
findings.summary: The Eighth Amendment to the Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984 (in force March 20, 2026) clarifies that beneficiary termination rights under Article 43 (the Jersey equivalent of Saunders v Vautier) are prohibited where other persons could become beneficiaries or where charitable/non-charitable purpose provisions exist; priority of claims between trustees and secured lenders is also clarified.
findings.client_impact: HNW families using Jersey trusts with discretionary or purpose provisions now have greater legal certainty that current beneficiaries cannot unilaterally collapse the trust structure, strengthening dynastic wealth protection; existing trust deeds should be reviewed in light of the clarified termination and lender-priority rules.
findings.urgency_reason: Amendment is in force since March 2026; no compliance deadline, but legal advisors should review existing Jersey trust instruments to understand how the clarified rules affect each specific structure.

findings.title: Guernsey Proposes ~20 Trust Law Amendments — Most Significant Reforms in a Generation
findings.summary: Proposition 21/2026, debated by Guernsey's States of Deliberation on May 20, 2026, proposes approximately 20 amendments to the Trusts (Guernsey) Law 2007, including reducing the minimum trustee number to one and permitting a trustee to transact with itself acting as trustee of another trust subject to safeguards. Advisors describe these as the most significant Guernsey trust law changes in a generation.
findings.client_impact: HNW families with Guernsey trust structures should track this legislation carefully; if enacted, the proposed changes will significantly increase flexibility in trustee arrangements and may affect the governance and liability framework of existing Guernsey trusts, potentially prompting deed reviews.
findings.urgency_reason: Amendments debated May 20, 2026 but not yet enacted; passage could occur in mid-2026, making early review of existing Guernsey trust structures prudent before the law changes.

findings.title: Guernsey GFSC: All Private Trust Companies Now Require Full Fiduciary Licence or Limited Permission — Since September 2025
findings.summary: Since September 2025, GFSC updated guidance requires all private trust companies (PTCs) operating in Guernsey to hold either a full fiduciary licence or a 'limited permission', closing a prior gap where some PTCs operated without formal licensing. This is particularly relevant to family-office PTCs used in HNW wealth structures.
findings.client_impact: HNW family offices using Guernsey-based PTCs as trustee vehicles must confirm that their PTC holds either a full GFSC fiduciary licence or a limited permission; operating without one is non-compliant and could jeopardize the legal integrity of associated trust structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Requirement has been in place since September 2025; any PTC still operating without the required licence is already non-compliant and requires immediate remediation.

findings.title: FATF February 2026 Plenary: BVI Remains Grey-Listed; Kuwait and Papua New Guinea Added
findings.summary: At the February 2026 FATF Plenary in Mexico City, Kuwait and Papua New Guinea were added to the list of Jurisdictions Under Increased Monitoring. BVI remains grey-listed (added June 2025). UAE and Panama remain off the grey list. The full grey list now includes 22 jurisdictions.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with structures in grey-listed jurisdictions — particularly BVI — face enhanced due diligence from counterparty banks and institutional investors globally; FGC should maintain a current FATF grey-list tracker and proactively flag any client structure domiciled in a newly or currently listed jurisdiction.
findings.urgency_reason: List is current as of February 2026 and will be updated at the next FATF Plenary; no single deadline, but client structure reviews for grey-list exposure should be conducted at least semi-annually.

findings.title: 2026 US Estate Tax: $15M Exemption for Citizens; $60K for Nonresident Aliens; FIRPTA Withholding Remains 15%
findings.summary: For 2026, US citizens and residents benefit from a $15 million federal estate tax exemption (inflation-adjusted under TCJA). Nonresident alien decedents retain only a $60,000 exemption on US-situs assets — including real estate — with estate tax rates up to 40%. FIRPTA withholding remains at 15% of gross price on foreign persons' dispositions of US real property, with an exception for sub-$300K residential sales.
findings.client_impact: Foreign HNW clients holding US real estate or other US-situs assets face a $60,000 estate tax exemption — far below the $15M afforded to US persons — exposing them to up to 40% estate tax on US assets above that threshold; this continues to make proper US estate tax planning (e.g., offshore holding structures for US real estate) essential for non-resident clients.
findings.urgency_reason: These are current-year parameters rather than new legislation; background awareness for wealth planning conversations, no immediate client action required.

findings.title: Cayman 2025-2026 NRA Factsheet Updated March 2026; CFATF 5th Round Evaluation Preparation Continues
findings.summary: The Cayman government issued an updated 2025-2026 NRA factsheet on March 2, 2026. The Inherent Risk Assessment component was extended by approximately five months to May 2026 for additional industry consultation ahead of the expected 2027 CFATF 5th Round Mutual Evaluation. CRS regime updates also took effect January 1, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Cayman service providers are actively improving their AML frameworks ahead of the 2027 CFATF evaluation; HNW clients with Cayman structures may see modestly increased CDD requirements from administrators, but no material change to client-facing obligations is expected in the near term.
findings.urgency_reason: Background preparation for a 2027 evaluation with no immediate client action required; useful context for understanding the direction of Cayman compliance standards.

findings.title: CIMA Fee Consolidation Effective January 1, 2026: FAR Fee Increases to ~US$548
findings.summary: Effective January 1, 2026, CIMA consolidated annual fees for regulated funds, replacing the prior dual annual fee plus annual return fee structure. The FAR fee for CIMA-Registered Funds increased from CI$350 to CI$450 (approximately US$548). Fee adjustments apply across several regulated sectors.
findings.client_impact: Cayman fund structures registered with CIMA will incur a modestly higher annual FAR fee from 2026; this is an administrative cost increase with no structural or compliance implications beyond the need to update fund budgets.
findings.urgency_reason: Administrative fee change already in effect; no planning action required, purely informational for cost management.

findings.title: MAS Highlights Private Banking Account Opening Workgroup to Improve Onboarding Efficiency
findings.summary: At the UBS Asian Investment Conference on May 25, 2026, MAS Managing Director Chia Der Jiun highlighted the Private Banking Industry Group's Account Opening Workgroup — co-led by MAS and industry — as a key initiative to streamline private banking onboarding while maintaining AML/CFT standards.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients opening new accounts at Singapore private banks may experience improved onboarding timelines as MAS and industry work to reduce friction without compromising compliance standards — a positive development for clients seeking Singapore private banking access.
findings.urgency_reason: Regulatory signal rather than a binding rule change; no client action needed, but useful context for clients planning to establish or expand Singapore private banking relationships.

findings.title: UAE National Committee Adopts New AML/CFT Initiatives — April 29, 2026
findings.summary: The UAE NAMLCFTC adopted new AML/CFT initiatives on April 29, 2026, building on the Decree-Law No. 10/2025 framework. Specific initiatives focus on enhanced enforcement mechanisms and inter-agency coordination; detailed measures were not fully disclosed publicly. UAE remains off the FATF grey list (removed February 2024).
findings.client_impact: UAE continues to strengthen its AML enforcement environment; HNW clients with UAE structures can expect continued high compliance standards, but the absence of specific new obligations in the April announcement means no immediate structural changes are required.
findings.urgency_reason: No specific new obligations disclosed; background context on UAE's ongoing AML posture with no immediate client action needed.

findings.title: Malta AML Framework: Financial Sector Fines Up to €5M Remain in Force
findings.summary: Malta's post-greylisting AML/CFT framework provides for administrative fines up to €5 million for financial sector entities and up to €1 million for non-financial sector entities for AML breaches, aligned with AMLD standards. Supervisory guidance was further reinforced in 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Malta investment funds or corporate structures should confirm that their Malta service providers maintain robust AML compliance programmes; the significant fine regime means service-provider non-compliance creates reputational and operational risk for clients.
findings.urgency_reason: Established framework with no new legislative change in this reporting period; background awareness of Malta's enforcement posture, no immediate action required.

findings.title: Bahamas New SAC Act Modernises Segregated Accounts Framework; DAO Legislation in Preparation
findings.summary: A new Segregated Accounts Companies (SAC) Act modernizes the 2004 framework, adding provisions for demergers, foreign continuations, and incorporated cells. Legislation for Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) is also in preparation.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients and fund managers using Bahamas segregated account structures benefit from a modernised framework with greater operational flexibility; the forthcoming DAO legislation may create new structuring options for clients with digital asset or Web3 ventures.
findings.urgency_reason: Positive legislative development with no immediate compliance obligation; background awareness of expanded Bahamas structuring options.

findings.title: Isle of Man Data Asset Foundations Act Receives Royal Assent May 20, 2026
findings.summary: The Isle of Man's Data Asset Foundations legislation received Royal Assent on May 20, 2026, creating a legal framework for data-as-an-asset relevant to digital wealth and tokenisation structures. The FSA also launched an AML/CFT guidance feedback survey on May 5, 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients exploring digital wealth or data-asset structures may find the Isle of Man an increasingly relevant jurisdiction for novel arrangements; practical application to specific client structures will require further legal analysis as the new framework beds in.
findings.urgency_reason: Legislative development for background awareness; no immediate compliance requirement and the framework is sufficiently new that practical guidance is still emerging.

findings.title: OECD Releases Pillar Two GMT Implementation Toolkit — April 30, 2026
findings.summary: The OECD Committee on Fiscal Affairs approved and declassified a comprehensive GMT Implementation Toolkit on April 30, 2026, providing practical guidance on legal implementation, IT systems, compliance procedures, taxpayer communications, and GIR data exchange for jurisdictions implementing Pillar Two rules.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients whose advisors operate across multiple Pillar Two jurisdictions will benefit from greater consistency as governments adopt the Toolkit, reducing the risk of conflicting local implementations that could create double-counting or duplicative filing obligations.
findings.urgency_reason: Guidance aimed at implementing jurisdictions rather than taxpayers directly; useful context for assessing cross-jurisdictional Pillar Two consistency but no direct client action required.
